content.text: Word2Vec is a family of shallow neural network models that learn dense vector representations of words from large text corpora. Published by Mikolov et al. at Google in 2013, Word2Vec was a breakthrough in natural language processing: for the first time, a simple and scalable method could produce word vectors that encode semantic and syntactic relationships with striking regularity.

Unlike count-based methods (TF-IDF, co-occurrence matrices) that produce sparse, high-dimensional vectors, Word2Vec produces dense, low-dimensional vectors (typically 100–300 dimensions) that can be used directly as features in downstream machine learning models. The training objective is predictive: the model learns to predict words from their context, and the word vectors emerge as a useful intermediate representation.

The two architectures — CBOW (Continuous Bag of Words) and Skip-gram — differ in which direction they make predictions but produce qualitatively similar embeddings. Word2Vec trained on large corpora (Google News, Wikipedia, Common Crawl) produces vectors that support vector arithmetic: the canonical example is king - man + woman ≈ queen, demonstrating that the model has implicitly learned gender as a direction in embedding space.

Before Word2Vec, representing words for machine learning required either one-hot encoding (sparse, no semantic information, vocabulary-sized vectors) or hand-crafted features that required significant domain expertise. One-hot vectors cannot express that "dog" and "puppy" are related — they are orthogonal in one-hot space regardless of their semantic similarity.

Word2Vec changed NLP by enabling transfer of word-level knowledge across tasks. A model trained on general text can provide word vectors that bootstrap domain-specific classifiers with far less labeled data. This was the first major demonstration of what is now called representation learning — learning general-purpose features from unlabeled data that transfer to supervised tasks. The insight directly motivated BERT, GPT, and modern large language models.
Cosine similarity between Word2Vec vectors reliably reflects semantic relatedness. "cat" and "kitten" are close; "cat" and "democracy" are far.
300-dimensional vectors versus 100,000-dimensional one-hot vectors; dense rather than sparse, making them efficient for downstream ML models.
Training uses raw text corpora. Any large text collection works as training data, enabling domain adaptation without labeled data.
Negative sampling reduces the softmax cost from O(|V|) to O(k) per step. Billion-word corpora can be trained in hours on a single machine.
Linear relationships in semantic space enable vector arithmetic for analogies, composition, and retrieval.
Context-free — one vector per word type.
"bank" (financial) and "bank" (river) share the same vector, averaged across all their contexts. Contextual embeddings (BERT, ELMo) solve this at higher compute cost.
Words not seen during training have no vector. FastText resolves this with character n-gram subword representations.
Biases in the training corpus are captured in the embeddings. Gendered profession associations (nurse=female, doctor=male) require debiasing post-processing.
Reliable embeddings require at least tens of millions of tokens; small corpora produce poor-quality vectors for rare words.
The Core Idea: Words as Vectors

Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) learns dense vector representations of words by training a shallow neural network to predict words from their neighbours. The learned vectors, called embeddings, capture semantic relationships: vectors for similar words end up close together, and arithmetic like
emerges naturally from the training objective.

Word2Vec offers two architectures. Both process a sliding window over a corpus and produce the same type of output — a weight matrix whose rows are word vectors — but they frame the prediction problem differently.

attrs.alt: CBOW (left): four context words are averaged into a hidden embedding that predicts the center word 'sat'. Skip-gram (right): the center word 'sat' is projected to an embedding that predicts each of the four context words independently. Comparison table shows CBOW is faster, Skip-gram handles rare words better.

content.text: CBOW takes the context words (the surrounding window) as input and predicts the center word. The context word vectors are averaged before the softmax output layer, which is why rare words tend to be diluted — their signal is mixed with the more frequent neighbours.
For a window of size 2 around the center word
using the averaged vector as input to a softmax over the full vocabulary.
Skip-gram reverses the task: given a center word, predict each surrounding context word independently. For a center word

Because each context word generates a separate training signal for the center word, Skip-gram produces more training examples per token. This makes it slower but gives better representations for rare words — each rare word still gets dedicated gradient updates rather than being averaged away.
Computing softmax over the full vocabulary (often 100 000+ words) is expensive. Negative sampling replaces it with binary classification: for each positive (word, context) pair, sample
random "negative" words and train the model to distinguish real pairs from noise. The objective becomes:
Negative words are sampled from the unigram distribution raised to the 3/4 power, which boosts rare words slightly. In practice
= 5–20 for small corpora, 2–5 for large ones.

# pip install gensim
from gensim.models import Word2Vec

# Corpus: list of tokenised sentences
sentences = [
    ["the", "cat", "sat", "on", "the", "mat"],
    ["the", "dog", "lay", "on", "the", "rug"],
    ["cats", "and", "dogs", "are", "common", "pets"],
    # ... typically millions of sentences
]

model = Word2Vec(
    sentences,
    vector_size=100,   # embedding dimensions
    window=5,          # context window size each side
    min_count=1,       # ignore words with fewer occurrences
    sg=1,              # 0 = CBOW, 1 = Skip-gram
    negative=5,        # number of negative samples
    epochs=10,
    workers=4,
)

# Access the embedding for a word
print(model.wv["cat"])          # shape (100,)
print(model.wv.similarity("cat", "dog"))   # cosine similarity
print(model.wv.most_similar("cat", topn=3))

# king - man + woman ≈ ?
result = model.wv.most_similar(
    positive=["king", "woman"],
    negative=["man"],
    topn=1,
)
print(result)   # [('queen', 0.87)] on a large trained corpus
For most projects there is no reason to train from scratch. Google's pretrained Word2Vec model (3 billion words, 300-dimensional, 3 million word vocabulary) is available as a binary file:

from gensim.models import KeyedVectors

# Download GoogleNews-vectors-negative300.bin.gz from the Word2Vec project page
wv = KeyedVectors.load_word2vec_format(
    "GoogleNews-vectors-negative300.bin",
    binary=True,
)

print(wv["python"].shape)          # (300,)
print(wv.most_similar("python", topn=5))
# [('ruby', 0.76), ('perl', 0.75), ('php', 0.73), ...]

import gensim.downloader as api

wv = api.load("word2vec-google-news-300")   # ~1.7 GB download, cached
print(wv.similarity("france", "paris"))     # 0.78
Smaller → syntactic similarity; larger → topical similarity
Discard rare words with fewer occurrences
: "bank" (financial) and "bank" (river) share the same vector. Context is ignored at inference time.
: words not seen during training have no vector. FastText solves this with character n-grams.
: embeddings mirror biases in the source corpus (e.g. gendered profession associations).
: morphologically related words ("run", "running", "ran") get unrelated vectors unless seen frequently together.
These limitations motivated contextual embeddings (ELMo, BERT) and subword-aware models (FastText, SentencePiece), which are covered in the next sub-subtopics.

Use Word2Vec (or load pretrained Word2Vec vectors) when: you need fast, static word representations for a traditional ML pipeline; you are working in a resource-constrained environment where transformer models are too large; or you need interpretable, lightweight embeddings for text classification, information retrieval, or semantic similarity in a domain-specific corpus.

Use contextual embeddings (BERT, RoBERTa, sentence-transformers) instead when: word sense disambiguation matters (polysemy is common in your domain), you need state-of-the-art accuracy, or your task involves understanding long-range dependencies within a sentence. For most modern NLP tasks with sufficient compute, contextual embeddings outperform Word2Vec.

Clinical NLP systems for medical coding, drug interaction detection, and patient note summarization require embeddings that understand medical terminology. General Word2Vec trained on Wikipedia does not reliably place "myocardial infarction" and "heart attack" close together. Training a domain-specific Word2Vec on clinical notes or PubMed abstracts produces embeddings where medical synonyms cluster correctly.

from gensim.models import Word2Vec
import re

# Simulate training on medical text (in production: PubMed abstracts or clinical notes)
medical_corpus = [
    "myocardial infarction is caused by blockage of coronary arteries".split(),
    "heart attack symptoms include chest pain dyspnea and diaphoresis".split(),
    "treatment of acute mi includes aspirin heparin and percutaneous coronary intervention".split(),
    "hypertension increases the risk of cardiovascular disease".split(),
    "diabetes mellitus is a risk factor for coronary artery disease".split(),
    "beta blockers reduce mortality after myocardial infarction".split(),
    "statins lower ldl cholesterol and reduce cardiovascular events".split(),
    # ... in production: millions of sentences from clinical notes
]

model = Word2Vec(
    medical_corpus,
    vector_size=100,
    window=5,
    min_count=1,
    sg=1,          # skip-gram: better for rare medical terms
    negative=5,
    epochs=100,    # more epochs for small corpus
    workers=1,
)

# Test: semantic similarity
print("Similarity: myocardial, heart:", model.wv.similarity("myocardial", "heart"))
print("Similarity: diabetes, coronary:", model.wv.similarity("diabetes", "coronary"))
print("Most similar to 'myocardial':", model.wv.most_similar("myocardial", topn=3))

# Document vectorization: average word vectors for document-level similarity
def document_vector(text: str, wv) -> "np.ndarray | None":
    """Average word vectors for words in vocabulary."""
    import numpy as np
    tokens = text.lower().split()
    vecs = [wv[t] for t in tokens if t in wv]
    return np.mean(vecs, axis=0) if vecs else None

note1 = "patient presents with chest pain and shortness of breath"
note2 = "acute myocardial infarction with dyspnea"
note3 = "routine checkup blood pressure normal"

import numpy as np
v1 = document_vector(note1, model.wv)
v2 = document_vector(note2, model.wv)
v3 = document_vector(note3, model.wv)

if v1 is not None and v2 is not None and v3 is not None:
    cos = lambda a, b: np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))
    print(f"Notes 1-2 similarity (cardiac): {cos(v1, v2):.3f}")
    print(f"Notes 1-3 similarity (cardiac vs routine): {cos(v1, v3):.3f}")
Word2Vec requires at least tens of millions of tokens for reliable embeddings. On small corpora, min_count should be lowered and epochs increased, but quality will still be limited.
Not normalizing before computing cosine similarity.
Gensim normalizes vectors internally for most_similar, but if you extract raw vectors for custom similarity, normalize them first.
Using CBOW for rare word tasks.
CBOW averages context vectors, which dilutes the signal for rare center words. Use Skip-gram for domains with technical vocabulary or rare terms.
"New York" should be a single token, not two. Use Gensim's Phrases detector to merge common bigrams before training.
Using Word2Vec for sentence similarity without averaging correctly.
Simple averaging of word vectors ignores word order and produces poor sentence representations. Use sentence-transformers for sentence-level similarity tasks.
Use pretrained vectors as a starting point.
Load Google News, GloVe, or FastText pretrained vectors and fine-tune on your domain corpus rather than training from scratch unless your domain is very specialized.
Apply subword models for morphologically rich languages.
FastText extends Word2Vec with character n-grams, handling OOV words and inflections automatically. Prefer FastText over plain Word2Vec for non-English languages.
Detect and encode phrases before training.
Use Gensim's Phrases class to convert bigrams and trigrams ("machine_learning", "new_york") to single tokens before training Word2Vec.
Use standard analogy benchmarks (Google analogy dataset) for intrinsic evaluation, but always validate on your downstream task — intrinsic scores do not always correlate with downstream performance.

Word2Vec with negative sampling trains in O(n · w · k) time per epoch, where n is corpus size in tokens, w is window size, and k is the number of negative samples. A 1B-word corpus with window=5, negative=5, vector_size=300 trains in roughly 30–60 minutes on 4 CPU cores. This is dramatically faster than count-based methods with SVD on the same data.

Memory requirements are modest: the model stores two matrices of shape (vocab_size × vector_size) — one for input embeddings and one for output embeddings. For vocab_size=500K and vector_size=300, this is 300MB of float32 — easily fits in RAM and can be loaded on edge devices.
Canonical Python implementation; supports KeyedVectors for inference-only loading
Handles OOV via character n-grams; better for morphologically rich languages
Preferred for sentence/document similarity; supersedes averaged Word2Vec
Integrates Word2Vec-style vectors in en_core_web_lg; easy pipeline integration
Fast most_similar at scale — query millions of vectors in milliseconds
Similar to Word2Vec; slightly better on analogies

Word2Vec embeddings inherit and amplify biases present in training data. A model trained on news articles from the early 2000s will encode historical gender, racial, and occupational stereotypes as geometric relationships in embedding space. The cosine similarity between "homemaker" and "woman" is higher than between "programmer" and "woman" simply because the training corpus reflects historical patterns. When these embeddings are used in downstream systems — resume screening, loan applications, content recommendation — the biases propagate into decisions that affect real people.

Detecting and mitigating embedding bias is a research-active area. The WEAT (Word Embedding Association Test) measures bias by comparing the relative distances between attribute words (e.g., male/female names) and target concepts (e.g., career/family words). Post-hoc debiasing methods (Bolukbasi et al., 2016) identify the gender direction in embedding space and project word vectors to remove the component along that direction, but complete debiasing without losing semantic utility is still an open problem.

# Measuring bias in word embeddings (WEAT-style)
import numpy as np

def cosine_similarity(v1: np.ndarray, v2: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return np.dot(v1, v2) / (np.linalg.norm(v1) * np.linalg.norm(v2))

def association_score(word_vec: np.ndarray,
                      attr_a: list, attr_b: list,
                      wv) -> float:
    """
    WEAT-style association: how much more is word_vec associated with
    attr_a words vs attr_b words?
    Positive score = more associated with attr_a.
    """
    a_vecs = [wv[w] for w in attr_a if w in wv]
    b_vecs = [wv[w] for w in attr_b if w in wv]
    if not a_vecs or not b_vecs:
        return 0.0
    mean_a = np.mean([cosine_similarity(word_vec, v) for v in a_vecs])
    mean_b = np.mean([cosine_similarity(word_vec, v) for v in b_vecs])
    return mean_a - mean_b

# Example: check if 'programmer' is more associated with male vs female names
# (would require a real trained Word2Vec model with these words in vocab)
# male_names = ["john", "james", "robert", "michael", "william"]
# female_names = ["mary", "patricia", "jennifer", "linda", "barbara"]
# career_words = ["programmer", "engineer", "doctor", "lawyer", "manager"]

# score = association_score(wv["programmer"], male_names, female_names, wv)
# print(f"'programmer' gender bias score: {score:.3f}")
# Positive = more male-associated; Negative = more female-associated

print("Bias measurement framework defined.")
print("In production: always audit embeddings for task-relevant biases before deployment.")

Word2Vec maintains two embedding matrices: W_in of shape (|V| × d) for input (center) word representations, and W_out of shape (|V| × d) for output (context) word representations. Only W_in is typically used as the final word vectors, though concatenating or averaging both can sometimes improve quality.
For Skip-gram with negative sampling, the update for a (center word w_I, context word w_O) pair with negative samples w_1, ..., w_k is:

attrs.latex: \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v_{w_i}} = \sigma(v_{w_i}^\top v_{w_I}) \cdot v_{w_I} \quad \text{for each negative sample } w_i

content.text: where σ is the sigmoid function. The gradient for the input word vector aggregates contributions from all context and negative sample updates:

The negative samples are drawn from the unigram distribution raised to the 3/4 power. This smoothing increases the probability of sampling rare words, preventing them from being perpetually under-represented as negatives:

where f(w) is the word frequency. The 3/4 exponent is empirically determined — higher values oversample rare words too aggressively, while lower values (like the unigram distribution at power 1.0) undersample them.
Implementing Skip-gram with Negative Sampling from Scratch

The following implementation trains Skip-gram with negative sampling without using Gensim, demonstrating the core mechanism. This is for educational purposes — in production always use Gensim or FastText.

import numpy as np
from collections import Counter

def sigmoid(x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))

class SkipGramNegSampling:
    """
    Minimal Skip-gram with negative sampling, from scratch.
    Educational — use Gensim for production.
    """
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, embed_dim: int = 50,
                 n_neg: int = 5, lr: float = 0.025):
        self.V = vocab_size
        self.d = embed_dim
        self.k = n_neg
        self.lr = lr
        # Two embedding matrices: input (W) and output (C)
        self.W = (np.random.rand(vocab_size, embed_dim) - 0.5) / embed_dim
        self.C = np.zeros((vocab_size, embed_dim))

    def train_pair(self, center_idx: int, context_idx: int, neg_idxs: list):
        """One gradient step for (center, positive_context, negatives)."""
        v_c  = self.W[center_idx]           # center word vector

        # --- positive sample ---
        u_o   = self.C[context_idx]
        score = sigmoid(u_o @ v_c)          # should approach 1
        err_c = v_c * (score - 1)           # gradient for C[context]
        err_w = u_o * (score - 1)           # accumulate for W[center]

        # --- negative samples ---
        for neg_idx in neg_idxs:
            u_n    = self.C[neg_idx]
            score  = sigmoid(u_n @ v_c)     # should approach 0
            err_c_neg = v_c * score
            err_w    += u_n * score
            self.C[neg_idx] -= self.lr * err_c_neg

        # Apply updates
        self.C[context_idx] -= self.lr * err_c
        self.W[center_idx]  -= self.lr * err_w

    def get_embedding(self, idx: int) -> np.ndarray:
        return self.W[idx]  # use input embeddings


# Build vocab and train on a tiny corpus
corpus = [
    "the cat sat on the mat".split(),
    "the dog lay on the rug".split(),
    "cats and dogs are pets".split(),
    "the mat is on the floor".split(),
]

# Build vocabulary
all_words = [w for sent in corpus for w in sent]
freq = Counter(all_words)
vocab = {w: i for i, (w, _) in enumerate(freq.most_common())}
idx2word = {i: w for w, i in vocab.items()}
freq_arr = np.array([freq[idx2word[i]] for i in range(len(vocab))], dtype=float)

# Negative sampling distribution (unigram^0.75)
neg_dist = freq_arr ** 0.75
neg_dist /= neg_dist.sum()

model = SkipGramNegSampling(len(vocab), embed_dim=10, n_neg=2, lr=0.05)

WINDOW = 2
EPOCHS = 500

for epoch in range(EPOCHS):
    for sent in corpus:
        for i, center in enumerate(sent):
            if center not in vocab:
                continue
            c_idx = vocab[center]
            for j in range(max(0, i-WINDOW), min(len(sent), i+WINDOW+1)):
                if j == i:
                    continue
                ctx_word = sent[j]
                if ctx_word not in vocab:
                    continue
                ctx_idx = vocab[ctx_word]
                # Sample negatives (avoid center and context)
                neg_idxs = []
                while len(neg_idxs) < 2:
                    neg = np.random.choice(len(vocab), p=neg_dist)
                    if neg != c_idx and neg != ctx_idx:
                        neg_idxs.append(neg)
                model.train_pair(c_idx, ctx_idx, neg_idxs)

# Test similarity
def sim(w1, w2):
    v1 = model.get_embedding(vocab[w1])
    v2 = model.get_embedding(vocab[w2])
    return np.dot(v1, v2) / (np.linalg.norm(v1) * np.linalg.norm(v2))

print(f"sim(cat, dog)  = {sim('cat', 'dog'):.3f}")   # should be moderate
print(f"sim(the, mat)  = {sim('the', 'mat'):.3f}")   # should be moderate
print(f"sim(cat, the)  = {sim('cat', 'the'):.3f}")   # should be lower
Word2Vec learns dense word vectors by training a shallow neural network to predict words from context (CBOW) or context from words (Skip-gram).
CBOW is faster and works well for frequent words; Skip-gram is slower but produces better representations for rare words.
Negative sampling replaces expensive full-vocabulary softmax with binary classification against k random noise words, enabling training on billion-word corpora.
The key limitation is that Word2Vec assigns one vector per word type — polysemous words ("bank", "lead") get a single averaged vector across all their meanings.
For most modern NLP tasks, contextual embeddings (BERT, sentence-transformers) outperform Word2Vec; use Word2Vec when efficiency and interpretability matter more than accuracy.
Use FastText instead of Word2Vec for morphologically rich languages or domains with many OOV words.
Word2Vec was the first large-scale demonstration of unsupervised representation learning for NLP, directly motivating the development of BERT, GPT, and modern LLMs.
